topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp white paper felt alien beneath Elias’s fingertips. He traced the elegant script detailing his life—the triumphs, the quiet sorrows, the very trajectory of his existence—and a cold knot tightened in his stomach. Every sentence was a carefully curated performance, a narrative he hadn't authored. He reread the final paragraph, where a description of his 'unwavering resolve' was penned with an almost mocking flourish. Elias leaned back in his chair, the leather groaning under his weight, his jaw tight. He stood up, pacing the length of his study, his footsteps echoing sharply on the hardwood floor. He needed answers, and he would find them, no matter how buried they were. He began methodically cross-referencing dates, searching for the ghost in the machine that had assembled this fiction.

[story 2]

A tremor ran through Clara’s hands as she unfolded the document. The biographical sketch was flawless, a tapestry woven with threads she didn't recognize as her own. A slow, heavy dread settled in her chest, making each breath shallow. She stared at the meticulous handwriting, the narrative voice speaking with an intimacy that felt invasive. Clara didn't move for a long time, just letting the silence of the room amplify the hollowness in her gut. When she finally spoke, her voice was low, almost a rasp. "Who wrote this?" The question hung in the air, sharp and demanding. She started gathering every piece of correspondence, every archived note, moving with a sudden, focused energy, her eyes darting across the room, cataloging potential witnesses and loose ends.

[story 3]

Marcus felt a distinct prickling sensation on the back of his neck as he reviewed the submitted biography. It wasn't just unfamiliar; it was a calculated fabrication, a complete rewriting of his personal history. A slow burn started in his chest, a simmering heat that demanded action. He slammed the file shut, the sound sharp enough to make the nearby desk rattle. Marcus walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent city lights, his posture rigid. He wasn't going to let this slide. He started making calls, his tone clipped and devoid of warmth as he pressed contacts, his mind racing ahead, constructing contingency plans. Every movement was deliberate, every glance toward the door an assessment of potential threats lurking just outside the frame.
